passage:
A clinical researcher compared the average recovery times (in days) of patients treated with three different therapies for a specific musculoskeletal injury, across two severity levels.

| Therapy | Mild Injury (days) | Severe Injury (days) |
|---|---|---|
| Physical Therapy | 18 | 42 |
| Surgery + Rehab | 31 | 28 |
| Rest and Medication | 22 | 58 |

The researcher concluded that physical therapy is the best first-line treatment for mild injuries, while surgery with rehabilitation is preferable for severe injuries.
question: Which finding from the table most directly supports the researcher's conclusion?

choices.A: Physical therapy has the shortest recovery time for mild injuries (18 days), and surgery with rehab has the shortest recovery time for severe injuries (28 days).
choices.B: Surgery with rehab takes 31 days for mild injuries, which is longer than both physical therapy (18 days) and rest and medication (22 days).
choices.C: Rest and medication has the longest recovery time for severe injuries at 58 days.
choices.D: Physical therapy recovers mild injuries 4 days faster than rest and medication.

explanation: The researcher's conclusion has two parts: (1) physical therapy is best for mild injuries, and (2) surgery with rehab is best for severe injuries. 'Best' here means shortest recovery time. Choice A directly supports both parts: for mild injuries, physical therapy has the shortest time (18 days); for severe injuries, surgery with rehab has the shortest time (28 days)—both statements using the same metric (recovery days) and comparing across all three options implicitly. Choice B supports only part of the conclusion (showing surgery is not best for mild injuries) but doesn't address severe injuries. Choice C addresses only one therapy for severe injuries without comparing to the others. Choice D provides a partial comparison for mild injuries only, and doesn't address severe injuries at all.
